_comment: Labeled set for the A1 routing evaluation. 'path' is the author-judged correct route: 'chat' for single-entity/definitional lookups answerable directly, 'agentic' for relational/multi-hop questions that need graph traversal. v2 (40 questions, balanced 20/20) is deliberately designed so QUERY LENGTH DOES NOT PREDICT THE LABEL: it includes long, verbose 'chat' questions (definitional but wordy) and short, terse 'agentic' questions (relational but compact). v1 was perfectly separable by word count (every chat <=5 words, every agentic >=8), which made a length heuristic unbeatable and meant no embedding/LDA router could ever win. v2 decouples intent from length so the task actually tests semantic routing. See data/routing_results.md and the README A1 section. Labels are still a judgment call; the point is comparing the supervised (LDA) router against trivial heuristics on the SAME labels. Balanced 20/20 so always-chat / always-agentic baselines cap at ~50%.

questions.text: Could you give me a thorough, careful, and reasonably detailed explanation of what exactly the BERT model is?
Please describe in plain language, with as much useful background as you can, what the Transformer architecture actually is.
Can you walk me through, in a fair amount of detail, what the core idea behind low-rank adaptation of large models is?
Give me a long, careful, textbook-style summary of what pre-training means in the context of language models.
Please explain, with plenty of background and as thoroughly as you reasonably can, what the GPT-3 model is.
In as much depth as you can manage, describe what Google Brain is and what kind of organization it represents.
What is Edward Hu known for?
Give a detailed, comprehensive definition of what a deep bidirectional Transformer encoder actually is.
Please provide a long and careful explanation of what few-shot learning means for large language models.
Which papers cite Attention Is All You Need, and what specific new ideas or techniques does each of those citing papers go on to introduce?
Taking into account their institutions, their authors, and the topics they cover, how are LoRA and the original Transformer paper related to one another?
Which authors are affiliated with OpenAI, and for each of them, what did they publish and how do those publications connect to the rest of the graph?
Walking through the citation graph step by step, what chain of papers and ideas connects the original attention mechanism to modern efficient-attention methods?
Considering authorship, institutional affiliation, and shared research topics all together, what is the full set of relationships linking GPT-3 to FlashAttention?
Connect Edward Hu to efficient attention.
Which institutions are connected to the authors of FlashAttention, and through what other papers or topics do those institutions also touch the rest of the corpus?
Starting from Ashish Vaswani, follow the graph through his paper, its topics, and the papers that build on it, and tell me everything that ultimately links him to LoRA.
Across authors, institutions, and topics, what are all the distinct paths through the knowledge graph that connect BERT to the FlashAttention paper?
Given everything in the graph, which two papers are the most densely connected to each other once you account for shared authors, shared institutions, and shared topics?
